| Mean-variance relationship | Confounded | Removed |
| Speed | Fast | Slower (v2 much better via glmGamPoi) |

## When to use SCTransform

- **Standard clustering/UMAP workflows** — it genuinely separates cell types better, especially when sequencing depth varies widely across cells (common in droplet-based data)
- Datasets where you want to regress out covariates (cell cycle, percent mito) cleanly during normalization
- Any time you're optimizing for biological resolution in embeddings

## When to stick with log-normalization

- **Differential expression** — this is the main "downstream issue" people mean. SCT Pearson residuals are not counts; running `FindMarkers` on the `SCT` assay gives wrong results. Always run DE on `RNA` assay with log-normalization regardless of which normalization you use for clustering.
- **Integration with non-Seurat tools** (Scanpy, scVI, etc.) — most expect log-normalized counts in a standard format
- **Very large datasets** where memory is a constraint (though Seurat v5 sketch integration helps here)
- **Simple integration** — log-norm + Harmony/RPCA is considerably simpler than `PrepSCTIntegration` + SCT

## The practical workflow in Seurat v5

```r
# Run both — they live in separate assays
obj <- NormalizeData(obj)          # populates RNA assay (keep for DE)
obj <- FindVariableFeatures(obj)
obj <- ScaleData(obj)

obj <- SCTransform(obj, vst.flavor = "v2")  # populates SCT assay

# Cluster using SCT
obj <- RunPCA(obj, assay = "SCT")
obj <- FindNeighbors(obj); obj <- FindClusters(obj)

# DE using RNA
DefaultAssay(obj) <- "RNA"
FindMarkers(obj, ident.1 = "cluster1", ...)
```

`vst.flavor = "v2"` is important — it uses glmGamPoi, is substantially faster, and is the current recommendation in the sctransform paper's follow-up.

## Bottom line

The "issues" people report are almost always either (1) running DE on SCT residuals, or (2) integration workflow confusion. If you keep the assay distinction clear — SCT for structure, RNA for statistics — SCTransform v2 is the better choice for most datasets.
reference:
Seurat's NormalizeData applies log1p(counts / library_size × 10,000), which does not stabilize variance across expression levels. Highly expressed genes retain far greater variance than lowly expressed genes after this transform, so PCA captures abundance differences rather than biologically informative variation.

SCTransform (sctransform v2, default in Seurat v5) fits a regularized negative binomial regression per gene using log10(total UMI) as a covariate and returns Pearson residuals. These residuals have approximately uniform variance across expression levels, improving signal-to-noise in dimensionality reduction. Additional technical covariates—percent mitochondrial reads, cell cycle scores—can be regressed out in the same call, avoiding a separate ScaleData step.

In practice, SCTransform improves clustering resolution and marker gene recovery for heterogeneous datasets. For relatively homogeneous datasets or datasets with very high sequencing depth, the gains are modest.

When NOT to use sctransform residuals: Pearson residuals are not counts and must not be passed to tools expecting count distributions. For differential expression after SCTransform-based clustering, call PrepSCTFindMarkers() to re-normalize using corrected counts stored in the SCT assay's counts layer—skipping this step produces miscalibrated p-values. Trajectory inference methods that require counts (e.g., Monocle 3 with count-based fitting) should use the RNA assay counts slot. SCTransform is also memory- and compute-intensive on datasets exceeding ~150k cells; consider sketch-based workflows in that regime.

input:
Reasons for extremely low number of DESeq identified differentially expressed genes after RNAseq?

I am fairly new at RNA-sequencing analysis, but I have attempted to analyse the data I obtained from RNA sequencing on my own by following an online EdX class and by basing the majority of the analysis on the recommendations of Azenta in the following guide: A Quick Start Guide to RNA-Seq Data Analysis .

For context, I am looking at making two different comparisons each time between human A549 cells treated with control siRNA (non-targeted siRNA) and a targeted knockdown using siRNA. I have 3 sequenced replicates for each condition.

As mentioned I mainly followed the steps recommended by azenta:

FASTQC to look at the quality of the samples (everything seems fine there)

Trimming reads with trimmomatic (ILLUMINACLIP:TruSeq3-PE.fa:2:30:10:2:keepBothReads LEADING:3 TRAILING:3 MINLEN:36)

Aligning the reads to the hg19 reference genome with STAR (the relevant files for the genome assembly, fasta and gtf, were retrieved from the gencode database)

Calculate gene hit counts with featureCounts (in R; isGTFAnnotationFile=TRUE, countMultiMappingReads=FALSE, GTF.featureType="exon", largestOverlap=TRUE, GTF.attrType="gene_id")

Compare hit counts between groups using DESeq2 with LFCshrink type Apeglem.

Without taking fold change into account, by only looking at the p-adjusted value < 0.05, we only identify 19 genes matching this parameter in one comparison and 2 in the other comparison. In each comparison, one of the genes that is identified as being differentially expressed is the one that was knocked down by our siRNA treatment.

We were expecting to identify much more differentially expressed genes, but since we do identify the knocked down gene, we are unsure whether something is wrong with our pipeline or with the samples? Or whether this could be interpreted as a "normal" amount of differentially expressed genes?

I tried different types of LFCshrink normalization which did not significantly alter the results. I also tried to run the easily accessible airway dataset through the DESeq2 pipeline, this dataset did seem to behave normally. I also tried a genome assembly based on files obtained from the UCSC database, which also did not alter the data.

Any guidance or advise would be appreciated to help figure out whether something is wrong with our analysis or not! Thank you for your help.
prediction:
This is a common source of confusion in RNAseq analysis. Your results are not necessarily wrong — here's a structured breakdown of how to think through this.

---

## The most important observation: your positive control works

You identify the knocked-down gene as differentially expressed. This means:
- Your library prep and sequencing worked
- Alignment is functioning
- featureCounts is counting correctly
- DESeq2 normalization is running properly

The pipeline is not broken.

---

## Likely biological explanation first

siRNA knockdown experiments are inherently "quiet" in terms of transcriptional consequences, unless the knocked-down gene is a **master regulator** (e.g., a transcription factor, chromatin remodeler, or major signaling hub).

If your target gene is, for example:
- A metabolic enzyme
- A structural/cytoskeletal protein
- A receptor with redundant family members
- A gene whose protein product acts post-transcriptionally

...then getting 2–19 DEGs is entirely plausible and may represent the true biology. The cell can compensate at the protein level without massive transcriptional reprogramming.

---

## Diagnostic checks to rule out technical issues

Run these to confirm it's biology and not a technical artifact:

**1. PCA plot**
```r
vsd <- vst(dds, blind=TRUE)
plotPCA(vsd, intgroup="condition")
```
This is the most important check. If your 3 replicates per condition don't cluster together tightly, high within-group variance is killing your statistical power. With only 3 replicates, DESeq2 has very limited ability to detect genes with noisy expression.

**2. Dispersion plot**
```r
plotDispEsts(dds)
```
Look for the dispersion trend fitting sensibly. If you see many outlier points well above the fitted line, your data is noisier than expected.

**3. MA plot**
```r
plotMA(res)
```
You want to see a symmetric cloud around 0 with shrinkage pulling low-count genes toward zero. If the plot looks asymmetric or very sparse, that tells you something.

**4. Check alignment and count statistics**
- What is your STAR alignment rate? It should be >85% for human samples.
- What is the total number of reads per sample? For DESeq2 to work well you typically want >10M reads per sample (>20M preferred).
- In featureCounts, what fraction of reads are "Assigned"? Low assignment (<60%) suggests annotation mismatches.

**5. Check size factors**
```r
sizeFactors(dds)
```
These should be reasonably similar across samples (e.g., 0.7–1.4 range). Very large differences suggest one library is an outlier.

---

## Potential technical issues to investigate

| Issue | How to check |
|---|---|
| Low read depth | Check STAR log: total reads, uniquely mapped reads |
| Poor replicate reproducibility | PCA clustering, sample-sample correlation heatmap |
| hg19 annotation mismatch | Your STAR genome and featureCounts GTF must be from the same source/version |
| Unintended off-target siRNA effects inflating variability | Hard to detect without more replicates |
| Cell passage number differences between replicates | Biological, not fixable in analysis |

---

## One specific pipeline note

You are using **hg19** (GRCh37), which is outdated. Gencode now primarily maintains GRCh38. This alone won't cause low DEG counts, but roughly 5–8% of annotated genes differ between assemblies, and some novel transcripts won't be captured. Consider moving to GRCh38 for future runs, though it won't dramatically change your current results.

---

## Bottom line

**19 DEGs in one comparison and 2 in another is consistent with a real but limited biological response to siRNA knockdown**, especially if the target gene is not a transcriptional master regulator. The best next step is the PCA plot — it will immediately tell you whether your replicates are behaving or whether noise is the culprit.
reference:
Generally, the recommended way of doing such analysis is exploring data by a dimensionality reduction technique such as PCA, e.g. implemented in DESeq2 via `plotPCA`. I personally always also look at MA-plots (`plotMA`). This will inform how samples cluster, and if there is evidence that some unwanted technical variation is present, or that samples are simply not different, or that normalization might be an issue. Without these plots one really cannot help here.

It is of course possible that there are no DEGs. No difference is not necessarily a technical error.

input: I'm running a neuronal RNA-seq experiment comparing two conditions (n=4 per group, 80M 150 bp paired-end reads, STAR alignment). I need to detect alternative splicing. Should I use rMATS or SUPPA2, and does my replicate count or read depth change the recommendation?
prediction:
## rMATS vs SUPPA2 for your experiment

**Short answer: use rMATS.** Your setup (STAR BAMs, n=4, 80M reads) is essentially the target use case it was designed for.

---

### Why rMATS wins here

| Factor | rMATS | SUPPA2 |
|---|---|---|
| Input | STAR BAM directly | Needs Salmon/kallisto re-quantification |
| Statistical model | Likelihood ratio test with overdispersion across replicates | Empirical/bootstrap dPSI — weaker at low n |
| Event types | SE, A5SS, A3SS, MXE, RI | Same + handles multi-event isoforms |
| Junction reads | Uses both read-on-junction and read-on-pair | Depends on accurate transcript quantification |
| Speed | Slower | Much faster |

Your STAR BAMs plug straight into rMATS — SUPPA2 would require a separate Salmon or kallisto run against a transcriptome reference, adding a step with its own accuracy tradeoffs.

---

### Does replicate count change the recommendation?

Yes, significantly. rMATS's LRT model explicitly estimates and accounts for biological variability across replicates. SUPPA2's empirical p-values are derived from within-group PSI variance, which becomes unreliable below ~5 replicates.

- **n=2:** Both tools struggle. rMATS is still preferable but power is low; report dPSI magnitude alongside significance.
- **n=3–4 (your case):** rMATS is clearly better. Its overdispersion parameter is estimable; SUPPA2's bootstrap is noisy.
- **n≥6:** Both become competitive. SUPPA2 gains appeal for speed and DTU analysis.

---

### Does read depth change the recommendation?

At 80M 150bp PE reads, depth is not your constraint — it's a strength for rMATS specifically:

- rMATS uses **junction-spanning reads** (reads crossing the splice junction) and **exon-body reads** separately. At 80M reads, even low-frequency neuronal alternative exons (which can have PSI < 0.1) will have sufficient junction coverage to be tested.
- SUPPA2 would also benefit from the depth (better transcript quantification), but you still have the replicate-count statistical issue.

If you were at 20–30M reads, junction coverage on rare neuronal events (e.g., microexons, poison exons) would be marginal for rMATS — depth matters more in that regime.

---

### Practical rMATS invocation for your setup

```bash
rmats.py \
  --b1 b1.txt --b2 b2.txt \   # text files listing BAM paths, comma-separated
  --gtf annotation.gtf \
  --od output/ \
  -t paired \                  # paired-end
  --readLength 150 \
  --nthread 16 \
  --tstat 16 \
  --variable-read-length       # recommended for 150bp with soft-clipping from STAR
```

Pass `--novelSS` if you want unannotated splice sites (useful for neuronal datasets where many exons are tissue-restricted and may be absent from generic GTFs).

---

### When to consider SUPPA2 additionally (not instead)

SUPPA2's **isoform switching** module (`diffSplice` in transcript mode) is complementary — it catches cases where overall isoform proportion shifts without a single clean splicing event. If you have a biologically interesting hit set from rMATS, running SUPPA2 on those genes as a follow-up is reasonable, not redundant.
reference:
At n=4 per group with 80M 150 bp paired-end reads, both tools are viable but differ fundamentally in what they model.

rMATS operates on aligned BAMs, counting reads that span or skip each of five event types (SE, A5SS, A3SS, MXE, RI) and testing ΔΨ with a likelihood-ratio framework. Your depth is adequate for moderate-to-high-abundance junctions, but low-coverage events — where the sum of inclusion and skipping junction counts is fewer than ~10 reads across most samples — should be filtered from the output post-hoc by thresholding the IJC and SJC columns. rMATS's statistical model degrades noticeably at n=2 per group; n=4 gives reasonable FDR control for expressed events.

SUPPA2 takes transcript-level abundances from Salmon or kallisto and computes per-event PSI, then tests ΔΨ across replicates using `diffSplice`. It's faster and integrates naturally into quantification pipelines, but it inherits Salmon's deconvolution ambiguity for closely related isoforms and cannot categorize events as granularly as rMATS.

Prefer rMATS when event-type resolution matters for validation or pathway annotation. Prefer SUPPA2 when you're already running Salmon and want exploratory speed. Neither tool reliably detects events where the minor isoform fraction is below ~5% of reads — for those, long-read sequencing is required. One practical note: all rMATS BAMs must use identical alignment parameters and annotation version to avoid systematic junction-count biases across samples.

input: We have 6 control vs 6 disease RNA-seq samples and are interested in changes in splice site choice — specifically which competing 5′ or 3′ splice sites are being selected differently, not just whether an exon is included. My PI says to use DEXSeq, but I've heard LeafCutter might be better here. When should I prefer one over the other?
prediction:
## DEXSeq vs LeafCutter: Choosing for Splice Site Competition

These tools model splicing fundamentally differently, and your question maps cleanly onto one of them.

### What each tool actually measures

**DEXSeq** bins reads into annotation-defined exon segments and tests whether each bin changes *relative to total gene expression*. It's fundamentally **exon-centric** — best for detecting whether a cassette exon is included or skipped.

**LeafCutter** counts split reads (junction-spanning) and groups them into **intron clusters**: sets of introns sharing at least one splice site. It then tests whether the *fraction* of each intron within its cluster shifts. This is fundamentally **intron competition-centric**.

### Why LeafCutter is the right fit for your question

Your question is exactly the LeafCutter design case. When you have competing 5′ SS, you get multiple introns that all share the same 3′ end — LeafCutter naturally clusters these together and tests which wins. Same for competing 3′ SS sharing a 5′ end. The Dirichlet-multinomial model explicitly asks: *given a read must choose among these competing junctions, is the choice distribution shifting?*

DEXSeq will struggle here because:
- Exon bins are annotation-derived — novel alternative splice sites are invisible
- It doesn't natively model the *competition* between two 3′ SS at the same donor; it tests each bin independently against total gene expression
- Distinguishing "donor shifted 10 nt downstream" from "exon skipped" requires inferring it from bin patterns, which is indirect

### When to prefer DEXSeq

| Scenario | Prefer |
|---|---|
| Cassette exon inclusion/skipping | DEXSeq |
| Competing 5′ or 3′ splice site choice | **LeafCutter** |
| Organism with poor splice junction annotation | **LeafCutter** (annotation-agnostic) |
| You need DESeq2-style size factor normalization across exons | DEXSeq |
| Integrating with an existing DESeq2/edgeR workflow | DEXSeq |

### For your specific setup (6 vs 6)

n=6 per group is workable for both, but LeafCutter's Dirichlet-multinomial dispersion sharing across clusters gives it reasonable power at this sample size — similar in spirit to how DESeq2 shares dispersion across genes.

### Practical suggestion

You can make a constructive case to your PI: DEXSeq and LeafCutter aren't competitors for your question — they answer *different* questions. If the PI wants exon-level sensitivity (which DEXSeq captures), run both and report concordant events; LeafCutter's cluster output maps naturally back to specific donor/acceptor coordinates that DEXSeq bins can corroborate. Framing it as complementary rather than a replacement tends to land better politically.
reference:
LeafCutter and DEXSeq address related but distinct questions, and your use case favors LeafCutter. DEXSeq tests whether individual annotated exon bins are differentially used relative to total gene expression — it's well-suited for cassette exon inclusion/exclusion events tied to a known transcript structure. It cannot detect changes at unannotated splice sites and does not model competition between alternative splice sites explicitly.

LeafCutter quantifies intron excision ratios within 'intron clusters' — groups of introns sharing at least one splice site — and tests for shifts in those ratios across conditions. Its key advantages for your goal: it directly models competition between 5′ and 3′ splice site alternatives within a cluster; it operates on splice junction reads rather than exon bins, so it naturally captures novel splice sites without annotation; and because it tests relative ratios within clusters, it is robust to expression-level confounders that would require separate modeling in DEXSeq.

With 6 vs 6, you should have adequate power for moderate effect sizes, but LeafCutter requires sufficient junction-spanning reads — aim for ≥50M paired-end reads per sample and confirm junction saturation curves have plateaued.

When NOT to use LeafCutter: if your primary question is quantifying inclusion levels of specific annotated cassette exons (e.g., to link findings to a known protein domain), DEXSeq's exon-bin framing and connection to annotated transcript structures is more interpretable. LeafCutter also struggles with single-exon genes and regions with very low intron diversity.

input: Beyond mapping rate and gene body coverage, what QC metrics should I prioritize specifically for alternative splicing analyses? I'm using STAR + rMATS on human tissue samples with ~80M paired-end reads each.
prediction:
For alternative splicing analyses with STAR + rMATS, the standard mapping rate and gene body coverage miss the most splicing-relevant failure modes. Here's what to prioritize:

## Junction-Level QC (highest priority)

**Splice junction saturation**
- Run `RSeQC junction_saturation.py` — at 80M reads you should be well past saturation for highly expressed junctions, but lowly expressed ones matter most for splicing
- If the curve is still steep at your read depth, you'll have high false-negative rates for rare exon-skipping events

**Novel vs. annotated junction ratio**
- Pull from STAR's `SJ.out.tab`: column 5 (uniquely mapping reads) and column 6 (multi-mapping)
- Flag samples where novel junctions exceed ~15–20% of total — suggests contamination, mapping errors, or sample mix-up
- Consistency across samples is more important than the absolute number

**Junction read support distribution**
- Minimum junction coverage is rMATS's most common failure point; check that the median reads per junction isn't dragging down toward rMATS's `--min-avg-intron-len` thresholds

## rMATS-Specific Pre-flight Checks

**Read length uniformity** — rMATS assumes consistent read length. Check with `fastp` or `seqkit stats`; post-trimming length variance will inflate SE/MXE noise.

**Strandedness confirmation** — run `RSeQC infer_experiment.py` and verify it matches your `--libType` flag. Wrong strandedness in rMATS silently destroys PSI estimates for antisense-heavy regions.

**Insert size distribution** — rMATS uses insert size for exon-skipping length filters. Get this from Picard `CollectInsertSizeMetrics`; multimodal distributions indicate a library prep problem that will skew short-exon detection.

## Alignment Flags Specific to Splicing

From STAR's `Log.final.out`, watch:

| Metric | Concern threshold |
|---|---|
| `% of reads mapped to too many loci` | >5% — multi-mappers confound PSI |
| `% of reads unmapped: too short` | >5% — suggests aggressive trimming removed junction-spanning reads |
| `Deletion rate per base` | >0.1% — hints at misalignment in repetitive intronic regions |
| `Mismatch rate per base` | >0.5% — RNA editing or sample degradation |

**Chimeric read rate** — if you ran `--chimSegmentMin`, check `Chimeric.out.junction`. High chimeric rates (>1%) indicate template switching during library prep, which rMATS can't distinguish from trans-splicing.

## Sample Comparability (often overlooked)

**Intron retention background** — use `IRFinder` or compute from STAR's `SJ.out.tab`. High IR in some samples but not others (e.g., from nuclear vs. cytoplasmic RNA fractions) will produce false differential splicing calls.

**Exon coverage evenness within transcripts** — `RSeQC read_distribution.py` broken down by feature type. Skew toward 3' exons indicates RNA degradation, which causes artifactual PSI changes at 5' splice sites.

**Between-sample PSI correlation on constitutive exons** — compute PSI for a set of known-constitutive exons (PSI should be ~0.95–1.0 in all samples). Outlier samples show processing or degradation problems that rMATS's FDR correction won't catch.

## Practical Priority Order

1. Strandedness verification (silent, catastrophic if wrong)
2. Junction saturation (determines power for lowly-expressed events)
3. Insert size distribution (affects exon length detection)
4. Novel junction ratio + chimeric rate (sample integrity)
5. Intron retention background (between-sample comparability)

At 80M reads you have good depth, so your main risks are systematic biases (strandedness, degradation gradients) rather than power — weight the QC accordingly.
reference:
Four checks are especially informative for splicing-focused pipelines:

**Junction saturation (RSeQC junction_saturation.py):** Downsample BAMs incrementally and plot novel vs known junctions detected. If the known-junction curve hasn't plateaued near your actual sequencing depth, some samples may be underpowered for reliable PSI estimation. Samples that diverge from the group trend here are candidates for exclusion or depth top-up.

**Inner distance distribution (inner_distance.py):** Bimodal distributions or unusual shifts suggest library prep problems — excessive RNA degradation or aberrant size selection — that cause non-random read placement near splice junctions and bias PSI estimates.

**SJ.out.tab non-canonical fraction:** Calculate the percentage of junctions with undefined or non-GT-AG motifs per sample. Healthy human samples should be >98% GT-AG. Outliers with elevated non-canonical rates indicate misalignment or a strand-specificity mismatch; confirm --soloStrand or --rnaStrandedness parameters are correct.

**rMATS event coverage audit:** After running rMATS, inspect the proportion of tested events filtered out for insufficient junction read coverage (the 'filtered' output files). A high exclusion rate often means read length is too short for reliable exon-flanking junction spanning — rMATS requires reads to span into both flanking exons, so a 75 bp library against complex exon structures can silently drop many events.

When not to over-weight saturation: for deeply sequenced samples (>150M reads) targeting abundant, well-spliced transcripts, saturation is rarely the bottleneck; focus instead on batch effects and RIN consistency across samples.

input: For a genome-wide methylation study (~850,000 CpG sites, n=48 cases and 52 controls), I'm debating between Bonferroni, Benjamini-Hochberg FDR, and Bonferroni-Holm correction. The goal is to identify candidate loci for a follow-up wet-lab experiment. Which should I use and why?
prediction:
For a discovery EWAS feeding into wet-lab follow-up, **Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) FDR** is the right choice. Here's the reasoning:

## Why not Bonferroni or Holm

Both control the **Family-Wise Error Rate** — the probability of even one false positive. That's the right criterion when a single false positive causes a catastrophic outcome (e.g., a drug approval decision). It is the wrong criterion here because:

- **Bonferroni threshold ≈ 5.9×10⁻⁸** (0.05/850,000). With n=100 and typical EWAS effect sizes (Δβ often <0.10), you'll have near-zero power. You'll likely identify nothing.
- **CpG sites are correlated** — co-methylation within CpG islands, shores, and shelves means your effective number of independent tests is far less than 850,000. Bonferroni treats all tests as independent, so it's doubly overcorrecting.
- **Holm** is uniformly more powerful than Bonferroni and still controls FWER, but it shares the same fundamental problem: the wrong error criterion for a discovery screen.

## Why BH FDR fits

BH controls the **expected proportion of false discoveries among your hits** — exactly the right tradeoff when:

1. **You have downstream validation**: false positives get eliminated in the wet lab. Missing a real locus (false negative) is the more costly error in discovery.
2. **Tests are positively correlated**: BH is theoretically valid under positive regression dependence (PRDS), which methylation data satisfies. Bonferroni's independence assumption is violated; BH's is not.
3. **It's field-standard**: EWAS pipelines (minfi, ChAMP) and most published EWAS use BH FDR, typically at q<0.05 or q<0.10.

## Practical calibration

| Scenario | Suggested threshold |
|---|---|
| Wet-lab is cheap (e.g., pyrosequencing panel) | q<0.10, Δβ>0.05 |
| Wet-lab is expensive (e.g., bisulfite sequencing) | q<0.05, Δβ>0.10 |
| BH gives no hits (common with n~100) | Suggestive p<10⁻⁵ as fallback |

With n=100 you may get few or no BH-significant hits if effect sizes are modest — this is a real power problem, not a multiple testing problem. In that case, report the top candidates by effect size with a suggestive p-value threshold and be explicit about the exploratory nature. Combining a p-value cut with a **Δβ filter** (biological effect size) is often more principled than p-value alone for wet-lab prioritization.

**Also check genomic inflation (λ)** before interpreting any of these — systematic batch effects or population stratification in methylation data can inflate the entire p-value distribution, making any correction misleading.
reference:
For discovery of candidates in a genome-wide screen with follow-up validation planned, Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) FDR is the standard choice. FWER methods like Bonferroni or Holm control the probability of any false positive across the test family—appropriate when each false positive has severe consequences (e.g., a clinical diagnostic) or when the family is small and well-defined. At 850K CpGs, the Bonferroni threshold (~5.9×10⁻⁸) is extremely conservative and will miss most true associations at this sample size.

BH-FDR controls the expected proportion of false discoveries among rejected hypotheses. It assumes tests are independent or satisfy positive regression dependency (PRDS), which approximately holds for CpGs on different chromosomes but may be violated for correlated probes in the same region. For arbitrary dependence structures, Benjamini-Yekutieli (BY) correction is valid but substantially more conservative.

A practical approach is BH at FDR<0.05 as the primary list, with a secondary exploratory list at FDR<0.20 explicitly labeled as unconfirmed. Avoid uncorrected p-values in genome-wide screens—inflated false discovery rates make replication nearly impossible to interpret.

Key caveat: BH at 5% still permits ~43,000 false positives if all 850K nulls are true, so effect size reporting and biological replication remain essential even after FDR control. FDR is a rate, not a guarantee per locus.

input: After running DESeq2 on my RNA-seq data (n=4 per group, 3 pairwise contrasts), I plotted the p-value histogram for each contrast and one of them looks roughly uniform with a slight anti-conservative spike near 0, while the other two have the expected L-shape. What does the uniform one mean and should I be worried?
prediction:
## P-value histogram interpretation

**The uniform + slight spike pattern is actually the "honest null" result** — not a sign of something broken.

### What each shape means

| Shape | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| L-shape (spike at 0, flat tail) | Many true positives; test is well-calibrated |
| Uniform + small spike near 0 | Few true positives; null genes behaving correctly |
| U-shape | Anti-conservative throughout — model misspecification |
| Spike near p=1 | Conservative test (independent filtering over-trimming, or outlier flagging) |

The uniform portion is the null genes. A perfectly flat uniform distribution *is* what correctly-behaved null p-values look like under H₀. The slight spike near 0 means a small number of genes are genuinely differentially expressed in that contrast — just fewer than in your other two. The test isn't broken; the biology is just quieter.

### Should you worry?

**Not about the shape itself.** But with n=4 it's worth a quick sanity check:

1. **Verify the contrast specification** — confirm the DESeq2 contrast vector or `resultsNames` entry points to the comparison you intend. A mis-specified contrast (e.g., comparing a group to itself, or wrong reference level) produces a uniform histogram with essentially no spike.
2. **Check sample metadata** — make sure no samples are swapped between the two groups in this specific contrast.
3. **Is the biology plausibly similar?** If these two conditions are closer to each other than to the third, less signal is expected and the uniform histogram is the correct answer.

### The one shape that *would* be worrying

A U-shape (excess of p-values near both 0 and 1) would indicate model problems — violated assumptions, batch confounding, or severe heteroscedasticity. You don't have that.

**Bottom line:** the uniform contrast likely just has little differential signal. The test is working. Confirm the contrast is specified correctly and that samples aren't mislabeled, then move on.
reference:
A uniform p-value histogram (flat across [0,1] with a modest spike near 0) tells you that most genes are null for that contrast — there is essentially no differential signal. The spike near zero represents the small fraction of truly differentially expressed genes. This is not a QC failure; it is biologically meaningful: that particular comparison may simply not have much signal given your experimental conditions and sample size.

The L-shaped histograms in your other contrasts are the expected healthy pattern: a large mass near 0 from true positives riding atop a flat null component.

What you should worry about instead: (1) A histogram that spikes near 1 or is bimodal suggests model misspecification — check for batch effects, outliers, or an inappropriate normalization. (2) A strongly anti-conservative distribution (mass shifted left of uniform) can indicate an inflated dispersion estimate or confounded design. (3) With only n=4 per group, the dispersion shrinkage in DESeq2 is doing heavy lifting; inspect your dispersion plot to confirm estimates are stable. For the low-signal contrast, BH-adjusted p-values will be poorly powered — consider reporting the raw effect sizes with confidence intervals rather than calling hits. Tools like `qvalue` can also estimate pi0 (the null fraction) formally, but with n=4 the estimate will be noisy.

input: In a clinical transcriptomics study I dichotomized continuous BMI into obese vs. non-obese at BMI=30 to include it as a covariate in my DESeq2 model. A reviewer is asking me to treat BMI as continuous. Does it really matter for a covariate, not for the main effect?
prediction:
The reviewer is right, and it does matter. Here's why, even for a covariate:

## Why dichotomization causes problems even for covariates

**Residual confounding.** A dichotomized variable doesn't fully absorb the BMI signal. Subjects at BMI=20 and BMI=29 are treated as identical; subjects at BMI=29 and BMI=31 are treated as maximally different. The within-category BMI variation remains uncontrolled and can still bias your main effect estimates — exactly the problem a covariate is supposed to solve.

**Loss of adjustment efficiency.** Dichotomization loses ~20% of the explanatory variance a continuous variable would capture (MacCallum et al. 2002, *Psych Methods*). In a GLM like DESeq2, this means more residual variance per gene, which reduces power to detect your main effect and inflates its standard errors.

**Propagated bias.** In DESeq2's NB-GLM, the coefficient for your main effect is estimated *conditional on* all covariates. If BMI is partially correlated with your main predictor (e.g., disease group, treatment), incomplete adjustment from dichotomization leaks into the main effect estimate — potentially shifting log2FCs and inflating or deflating p-values.

**The cutoff is arbitrary.** BMI=30 is a clinical threshold, not a statistical one. The true BMI–expression relationship almost certainly doesn't have a discontinuity there.

## The practical impact depends on

- How much BMI correlates with your main predictor in this sample
- How spread your BMI distribution is around 30 (concentrated near the cutoff = worse)
- Whether the BMI–expression relationship is linear or step-like (it almost certainly isn't a step)

## What to do

Rerun DESeq2 with `~ BMI_continuous + your_main_effect`. If you're worried about outliers or a skewed distribution, consider `log(BMI)` or check the BMI distribution first. Running both as a sensitivity analysis is also reasonable for the rebuttal — if results are highly concordant, you can say so; if they differ, the continuous model is more defensible.

The standard line "it's just a covariate" doesn't hold statistically: covariate misspecification propagates directly into the estimates and inference for your main effect.
reference:
Yes, it matters even for a covariate, and the reviewer is correct. Dichotomizing a continuous variable discards information about variation within each bin — two subjects at BMI 30.1 and BMI 45 are treated identically, even though their transcriptomic profiles likely differ substantially. This information loss reduces the precision of the BMI coefficient estimate, which in turn leaves more residual variance in the model. More residual variance means larger standard errors for your primary contrast of interest, lower effective power, and potentially biased effect estimates if BMI has a non-uniform relationship with expression across the range.

The statistical literature is clear on this: MacCallum et al. (2002) and others showed that median-splitting a continuous predictor is equivalent to discarding roughly 20% of your sample. Using BMI=30 as a threshold also introduces an arbitrary breakpoint that may not reflect the biological dose-response.

In DESeq2, adding a continuous covariate is straightforward: `~ BMI_continuous + condition`. If you're concerned about non-linearity, you can add a spline term or check residual plots. The continuous form requires no binning decision and is more robust to outliers than a hard threshold.

Caveat: if your sample is heavily clustered at extreme BMI values with a sparse middle range, a linear term may be a poor fit and a sensitivity analysis with restricted cubic splines is warranted. But even then, the answer is a more flexible continuous model, not dichotomization.

input: I have an RNA-seq experiment with 3 conditions (control, drug A, drug B) and I'm running DESeq2. Should I correct for multiple testing across all three pairwise contrasts together, or run FDR correction within each contrast separately? My collaborator says to pool everything, but that seems wrong.
prediction:
Your instinct is right. Correct within each contrast separately — which is also what DESeq2 does by default.

**Why per-contrast is correct:**

Each pairwise contrast is answering a distinct biological question. The BH-adjusted p-value from a contrast means: "among genes I call DE in *this comparison*, what fraction are expected false positives?" That's a meaningful, interpretable claim. Pooling destroys that interpretability.

**Why pooling is problematic:**

- The same gene appears in all three contrasts, so pooling triple-counts it. BH assumes the hypotheses are at least weakly independent — repeated entries for the same gene violate that.
- After pooling, an FDR of 5% means 5% false discoveries across a mixture of three different biological questions. You can't then say "in the control-vs-drugA list, 5% are false" — that statement is no longer supported.
- It would also give better-looking adjusted p-values to genes significant in multiple contrasts, for no principled reason.

**When your collaborator's concern is valid (but the solution isn't pooling):**

If you're asking "did I just go fishing across three comparisons to find *something* significant?", that's a legitimate multiplicity concern. But the answer is to think carefully about your primary hypothesis upfront and perhaps apply methods like IHW, not to concatenate p-value lists.

**Practical confirmation:**

When you call `results(dds, contrast=c("condition","drugA","control"))`, DESeq2 applies BH to that contrast's ~20k p-values. Repeat for the other two contrasts. Each gene list has its own FDR guarantee. This is the field-standard approach and what the DESeq2 paper describes.

Your collaborator may be confusing this with situations where you truly have one pooled test (e.g., an omnibus F-test across all conditions), which is a different thing entirely.
reference:
Your instinct is correct — pooling contrasts for FDR correction is almost always wrong. DESeq2 already applies Benjamini-Hochberg (BH) correction per contrast internally, and the q-value for a gene in the 'drug A vs control' contrast should reflect the number of hypotheses tested within that comparison, not inflated by an unrelated 'drug B vs control' test. Pooling would dilute your power: genes that are significant in one contrast but not others would have their ranks worsened by the additional noise.

The legitimate case for a joint correction arises when you define a single biological question spanning contrasts — for example, 'is this gene DE in any condition?' — and you want family-wise error control over that combined claim. In that framing, using results from a likelihood-ratio test (DESeq2's `test='LRT'`) with a reduced model is cleaner than pooling p-values post-hoc.

One genuine caveat: if you run many exploratory contrasts and cherry-pick the most significant, per-contrast FDR no longer protects the experiment-level error rate. In that case, consider reporting which contrasts were pre-specified versus exploratory. Never correct across contrasts just to make results look more conservative — it destroys power without a principled justification.

input: I have an RNA-seq experiment with 6 conditions (2 genotypes × 3 timepoints, n=3 biological replicates each). I'm running DESeq2 and plan to test 15 pairwise contrasts. Should I correct for multiple testing across all contrasts jointly, or is per-contrast BH adjustment acceptable? Does DESeq2 do this automatically?
prediction:
## Multiple Testing in DESeq2 Across Contrasts

### What DESeq2 does automatically

DESeq2 applies BH (Benjamini-Hochberg) FDR correction **per contrast, across genes** — each call to `results()` independently adjusts p-values for the ~20,000 genes in that contrast. It does **not** correct across multiple contrasts.

```r
res1 <- results(dds, contrast = c("condition", "A", "B"))  # BH over genes, for this contrast only
res2 <- results(dds, contrast = c("condition", "A", "C"))  # separate BH, unaware of res1
```

### Per-contrast BH is the field standard — and appropriate here

Correcting jointly across all 15 contrasts is **not standard practice** and would be overly conservative for two reasons:

1. **Each contrast is a distinct biological hypothesis.** The FDR framework is most meaningful when applied to a single family of tests sharing a common null. Pooling 15 × N_genes p-values treats unrelated comparisons as one family.
2. **Your contrasts are not independent** (shared samples within genotypes/timepoints), which invalidates simple joint BH and makes the correction harder to interpret.

Per-contrast BH at `padj < 0.05` is what virtually all published RNA-seq studies do, and it is what reviewers expect.

### A more important concern for your design

With a 2×3 factorial, running all 15 pairwise contrasts ad hoc is statistically and interpretively weaker than using your model structure. Consider:

```r
# Full interaction model
dds <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(..., design = ~ genotype + timepoint + genotype:timepoint)
dds <- DESeq(dds)

# LRT to identify any genes with a genotype:timepoint interaction
dds_lrt <- DESeq(dds, test = "LRT", reduced = ~ genotype + timepoint)
res_interaction <- results(dds_lrt)  # single well-powered test for the interaction
```

This is more powerful because:
- The LRT pools information across all groups to estimate dispersion
- You get a single, well-controlled FDR for the interaction effect
- You then extract specific contrasts only for genes that pass the LRT — reducing the number of follow-up tests

### Summary

| Question | Answer |
|---|---|
| Does DESeq2 correct across contrasts? | No — per contrast only |
| Is per-contrast BH acceptable? | Yes, it's the standard |
| Is joint correction required? | No, and it would be anti-conservative to your power |
| Better approach for 2×3 factorial? | LRT for interaction + targeted follow-up contrasts |

The one situation where joint correction across contrasts matters is if you're making a **gene-level claim of the form "DE in at least one comparison"** and treating the union as a discovery list — in that case, use `IHW` or manually apply BH to the minimum per-gene p-value across contrasts. But for standard contrast-by-contrast reporting, per-contrast BH is correct.
reference:
DESeq2 applies BH correction independently within each contrast — it does not jointly correct across contrasts. Whether per-contrast adjustment is acceptable depends on how you frame your inference goal.

Per-contrast BH is the standard practice when each contrast addresses a distinct biological question (e.g., 'what is DE between WT day1 and WT day3'). The family-wise error rate (FWER) is not controlled across all 15 contrasts, but the FDR within each is. This is what most published workflows do and is appropriate when readers interpret each contrast on its own terms.

If your primary goal is a single list of genes that are DE under *any* comparison — for example, feeding into a downstream pathway analysis — you should be more conservative. One option is to test an omnibus LRT (likelihood ratio test) across the full model versus a reduced model; this gives a single well-calibrated p-value per gene for the global null and avoids the multiplicity problem entirely. DESeq2 supports this via `test='LRT'`.

What you should not do: run 15 contrasts, pool all resulting p-values into one vector, and BH-correct the pool. The null distributions differ across contrasts (different effect sizes, different baselines), so a pooled correction is neither conservative nor interpretable.

Caveat: with n=3 per group, power is low for any individual contrast. Prioritize biologically motivated comparisons rather than exhaustively testing all 15.
